refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires clarification of scope (what constitutes 'worksite model'), measurement basis (p95 latency across which scenarios), and customer-specific acceptance criteria. The refined FR explicitly bounds the latency requirement to a statistical percentile and acknowledges customer tailoring, critical for high-safety-criticality systems where deterministic guarantees matter.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument worksite model update pipeline with end-to-end latency telemetry; measure p95 latency across representative scenarios (equipment state change, sensor event, tele-operator command, safety alert); compare against customer acceptance criteria; validate under nominal and degraded network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Capture and transmit equipment state changes (position, orientation, operational mode) with minimal local processing latency.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Receive and integrate transmitted state updates into the worksite model with bounded processing and storage latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Propagate worksite model updates to all authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) within the latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Ensure latency compliance under network congestion, packet loss, and multi-equipment scenarios.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model server with real-time event streaming; all equipment pushes telemetry to a single model authority; consumers subscribe to model change events.
DP-B: Distributed edge-cached worksite model with eventual consistency; each equipment maintains a local model replica; periodic sync with authoritative server; consumers read from nearest replica.

DP-C: Hybrid hierarchical model: Vehicle maintains local state; Teleoperation and Safety Services maintain synchronized replicas via publish-subscribe; latency-critical updates bypass model server and go directly to Safety Services.
DP-D: Time-synchronized state broadcast: Equipment broadcasts state snapshots at fixed intervals; all consumers receive and integrate independently; no central model authority.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid hierarchical model with publish-subscribe event streaming and latency-critical safety bypass.

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle state capture and Teleoperation/Safety Services subscription logic (mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface).
Coupling between model update frequency and network bandwidth (mitigated by selective update filtering at Vehicle level).
Coupling between safety-critical update bypass and eventual consistency guarantees (mitigated by explicit safety alert prioritization in Safety Services).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples the worksite model authority (Vehicle) from consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services) via publish-subscribe, reducing latency variance. Safety-critical updates bypass the model server, ensuring p95 latency for safety alerts. Eventual consistency is acceptable for non-safety state; strong consistency is enforced for safety-relevant state via SafetyAlert interface.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network Link supports publish-subscribe semantics (or can be abstracted to do so).
Assumption: Vehicle can capture and timestamp state changes with sub-100ms granularity.
Risk: If network latency dominates (e.g., high-latency satellite link), p95 target may be unachievable without local model caching at consumer sites.
Risk: Eventual consistency in non-safety state may cause brief misalignment between Teleoperation and Safety Services views; requires explicit reconciliation logic.
Risk: Safety bypass path must be protected against unauthorized or malformed updates; requires authentication and validation in Safety Services.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network topology and latency budget allocation: (1) Vehicle → Network Link latency, (2) Network Link → Teleoperation/Safety Services latency, (3) local processing latency at each consumer. If network latency exceeds 500 ms, recommend edge caching (DP-B hybrid) to meet p95 ≤ 1.0 s.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Teleoperation module owns the worksite model update reception, integration, and propagation logic. It subscribes to Telemetry and SafetyAlert streams from Vehicle and Safety Services; maintains a synchronized replica of the worksite model; and exposes the current model state to tele-operator UI and external consumers via Tasking interface (query/subscribe). Teleoperation also coordinates with Safety Services to ensure safety-critical updates are prioritized.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives equipment state updates (position, orientation, operational mode, sensor readings) from Vehicle at configurable frequency; used to update worksite model replica.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical state changes and alerts from Safety Services; prioritized for immediate model update and propagation to tele-operator.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Exposes worksite model query and subscription interface to tele-operator UI and external consumers; receives tele-operator commands and returns current model state with latency metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying transport for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking; Teleoperation module is agnostic to network topology but depends on Network Link to meet latency SLA.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container; worksite model update is a system-wide concern spanning Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and make latency ownership unclear. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle owns state capture and initial transmission (Telemetry interface). However, model integration, replica management, and propagation to tele-operator are outside Vehicle scope. Vehicle should not own the worksite model; it should own only the source telemetry stream.

Teleoperation is the primary consumer of worksite model updates and the logical owner of the model replica and update integration logic. Teleoperation has direct responsibility for tele-operator situational awareness, which depends on timely model updates. Latency ownership is clear: Teleoperation must integrate updates within its processing budget. High containment, low diffusion. Recommended primary owner.

Safety Services owns safety-critical state and alerts (SafetyAlert interface). It should not own the general worksite model, but it should own the safety-relevant subset and ensure its updates bypass latency-sensitive paths. Shared responsibility with Teleoperation for safety state synchronization.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of worksite model update latency because: (1) it is the primary consumer of Telemetry and SafetyAlert streams, (2) it maintains the model replica used by tele-operator UI, (3) it has direct control over model integration and propagation logic, (4) latency ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation must deliver updates to tele-operator within the p95 budget. Vehicle owns source telemetry; Safety Services owns safety-critical state; Teleoperation orchestrates the integration.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle generates telemetry faster than Teleoperation can integrate, buffering and backpressure logic must be implemented in Teleoperation; this could increase latency variance.
Risk: Teleoperation depends on Network Link latency, which is outside its control. If network latency is high, Teleoperation cannot meet p95 target without local caching or edge processing.
Risk: Teleoperation must synchronize with Safety Services for safety-critical state; if Safety Services updates are delayed, Teleoperation's model may diverge from safety ground truth.
Risk: Multiple tele-operators or external consumers querying Teleoperation's model replica could create contention; requires thread-safe or event-driven access patterns.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish clear latency budget allocation: (1) Vehicle → Network Link: ≤ 300 ms (p95), (2) Network Link → Teleoperation: ≤ 300 ms (p95), (3) Teleoperation processing: ≤ 200 ms (p95), (4) Teleoperation → Tele-operator UI: ≤ 200 ms (p95). Total: ≤ 1.0 s. Define backpressure and buffering strategy in Teleoperation to handle telemetry bursts. Establish synchronization protocol between Teleoperation and Safety Services for safety-critical state.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid hierarchical model with publish-subscribe) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for p95 latency ≤ 1.0 s. The DP decouples state capture (Vehicle) from model integration (Teleoperation) and safety propagation (Safety Services), enabling independent latency optimization. Safety-critical bypass ensures SafetyAlert updates meet strict latency bounds. The DP is feasible, testable, and aligns with high-safety-criticality constraints.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Telemetry stream processing logic could be duplicated across Teleoperation and Safety Services if not carefully abstracted. Recommend a shared Telemetry adapter or middleware layer.

Diffusion risk: Model synchronization logic between Teleoperation and Safety Services could diverge if not governed by a common protocol. Recommend explicit synchronization interface (e.g., SafetyAlert includes model state snapshot).

Diffusion risk: Latency instrumentation and monitoring must be consistent across Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Recommend centralized telemetry collection in Teleoperation or a dedicated monitoring module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module contains the worksite model replica and update integration logic, providing clear ownership and accountability for latency. Vehicle and Safety Services are external producers; Teleoperation is the integrator and distributor. This containment is appropriate for a high-safety-criticality system where model consistency and latency are critical.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented end-to-end latency measurement: (1) timestamp telemetry at Vehicle source, (2) timestamp at Teleoperation reception, (3) timestamp at tele-operator UI display. Measure p95 latency across representative scenarios (single equipment, multi-equipment, network congestion, safety alert). Acceptance criteria: p95 latency ≤ customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency allocation is clear: Vehicle owns source telemetry latency (≤ 300 ms), Network Link owns transport latency (≤ 300 ms), Teleoperation owns integration and propagation latency (≤ 200 ms), Tele-operator UI owns display latency (≤ 200 ms). Each module has explicit latency budget and can be tested independently.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation in DP: specify p95 latency targets for each stage (Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation, UI).
Define backpressure and buffering strategy in Teleoperation to handle telemetry bursts without exceeding latency budget.
Establish synchronization protocol between Teleoperation and Safety Services for safety-critical state; specify consistency guarantees (strong vs. eventual).
Define latency instrumentation and monitoring strategy; recommend centralized telemetry collection.
Clarify network topology and latency assumptions; if network latency is high, recommend edge caching or local model replication at consumer sites.

decision.justification: The refined FR is decomposable into four sub-FRs (state capture, model integration, propagation, and latency compliance under degraded conditions). DP-C is sound and well-aligned with the FR, but it requires further decomposition to address latency budget allocation, backpressure handling, and safety-critical synchronization. Teleoperation is the appropriate IM owner, but its internal architecture (buffering, event dispatch, model replica consistency) must be designed in the next level. Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs will clarify implementation details and reduce latency risks.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Capture and transmit equipment state changes (position, orientation, operational mode) with minimal local processing latency (Vehicle responsibility).
FR-NGHE-007.2: Receive and integrate transmitted state updates into the worksite model with bounded processing and storage latency (Teleoperation responsibility).
FR-NGHE-007.3: Propagate worksite model updates to all authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) within the latency budget (Teleoperation responsibility).
FR-NGHE-007.4: Ensure latency compliance under network congestion, packet loss, and multi-equipment scenarios (Teleoperation + Safety Services responsibility).

assumptions: Network Link supports publish-subscribe semantics or can be abstracted to do so.
Vehicle can capture and timestamp state changes with sub-100 ms granularity.
Tele-operator UI can render model updates within 200 ms of reception.
Safety Services can process and propagate safety-critical alerts within 100 ms.
Customer-defined latency target is 1.0 s p95 (or will be specified during requirements refinement).
Worksite model includes equipment position, orientation, operational mode, and safety-relevant state; does not include high-frequency sensor streams (e.g., raw accelerometer data).
open_risks: Network latency may dominate the p95 budget; if network latency exceeds 500 ms, p95 ≤ 1.0 s may be unachievable without edge caching.
Eventual consistency in non-safety state may cause brief misalignment between Teleoperation and Safety Services views; requires explicit reconciliation logic.
Telemetry stream processing logic could be duplicated across modules if not carefully abstracted.
Latency instrumentation and monitoring must be consistent across all modules; risk of measurement inconsistency.
Safety-critical bypass path must be protected against unauthorized or malformed updates; requires authentication and validation.
evidence_needed: Network latency profile: measure p95 latency between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services under nominal and degraded conditions.
Vehicle telemetry generation rate and latency: measure time from state change to telemetry transmission.
Teleoperation model integration latency: measure time from telemetry reception to model update completion.
Tele-operator UI rendering latency: measure time from model update to display update.
Safety Services alert propagation latency: measure time from safety event to SafetyAlert transmission.
Multi-equipment scenario latency: measure p95 latency with 5, 10, 20 concurrent equipment units.
Network congestion scenario latency: measure p95 latency under 50%, 75%, 90% network utilization.
Customer acceptance criteria: confirm customer-defined latency target and supported update scenarios.
